file_insights.purpose: Hilt dependency injection module for scanner-related dependencies

patterns_discovered.description: Uses Hilt @Module with @InstallIn to provide dependency for application lifetime
Provides single instance of NewlandScannerManager scoped to SingletonComponent
Uses @ApplicationContext qualifier for application-level context injection
Uses Kotlin object keyword for singleton module pattern
Includes detailed documentation about module purpose, lifecycle, and scanner functionality

gotchas_discovered.description: NewlandScannerManager registers BroadcastReceivers. Module provides singleton instance but cleanup must be handled by NewlandScannerManager or application lifecycle aware component
gotchas_discovered.mitigation: Verify NewlandScannerManager implements proper unregistration in cleanup methods

gotchas_discovered.description: Scanner manager lives for entire application lifetime. Any state holding could impact memory usage
gotchas_discovered.mitigation: Monitor for memory leaks, particularly with BroadcastReceiver and SharedFlow emissions

approach_outcome.implementation_strategy: Created Hilt DI module with provider method following Android dependency injection best practices

recommendations.recommendation: Verify NewlandScannerManager properly unregisters BroadcastReceivers to prevent memory leaks
recommendations.rationale: Singleton objects with registered broadcast receivers can leak if not cleaned up properly

recommendations.recommendation: Consider adding @BindsInstance or test binding variants for unit testing NewlandScannerManager
recommendations.rationale: Facilitates mock injection during testing phases

recommendations.recommendation: Document cleanup/lifecycle requirements for NewlandScannerManager in module KDoc
recommendations.rationale: Helps future maintainers understand scope implications and cleanup responsibilities

recommendations.recommendation: Monitor SharedFlow emissions mentioned in comments for backpressure handling
recommendations.rationale: Ensure reactive event emission doesn't cause issues in high-throughput scanning scenarios
